the-coat-i-left-on-the-rail: I wrote this one in the present tense on purpose, even though everything about it is already decided by the time the song starts. The coat detail came from an actual coat I almost left at my mother's house once and didn't, and I've regretted the not-leaving more than most things I've actually done. The steward who keeps asking if she's cold isn't a real person in my draft — he started as a stage direction, a way to get the line about pretending into the room, and I liked him enough to keep him nameless and minor. I cut a whole verse about the gangway because it explained too much. The not-explaining is the song.
letters-i-didnt-post: Eleven letters because ten felt round and round felt like I was making a point. I wanted an ugly, specific number, like she'd actually counted them obsessively in a bunk at 2am. The porthole-as-clock-with-no-time line took me three days and I almost threw the whole song out trying to get there — everything before it in that verse is scaffolding I built just to reach one image. I'll admit the twine is autobiographical; I tie things with twine I have no intention of ever opening, and I've always wondered what that says about me. I let the sea take six letters and never decided which six. That felt more honest than choosing.
the-name-i-gave-the-customs-officer: The false name in the song is never given to the listener, which was a fight with myself for weeks — I wanted to write the name down, prove I'd thought of one, and then I realized the whole point was that it doesn't matter what the name is, only that she answered to it too fast. The detail of writing it on the back of her hand came from watching someone do exactly that at an airport once, just rehearsing their own cover story in ballpoint. The two lamps in the hallway at the end were originally one lamp. I added the second because a single lamp felt resolved, and she isn't.
the-last-photograph-before-the-pier: I counted buttons on a stranger's collar in an old photo of my own family for an embarrassingly long time before I wrote this, trying to figure out why we count things we're losing. Five buttons, one gull — I needed both numbers to be small and exact, because grief that's measured in fractions of inches feels more true to me than grief that's measured in feelings. The bridge is the shortest thing on the record and I almost cut it for being too bare. I left it in because the photograph itself doesn't get a bridge, doesn't get a long examination — it just gets handled until it softens, same as the verse does to it.
cargo-we-dont-name: Crate forty-six wasn't always forty-six. It went through nine, twelve, thirty-one before I landed on a number with no symbolic weight at all, because symbolic numbers would have tipped the song into allegory and I wanted it to stay a job, a boat, a woman trying not to think about what she heard. The knocking pattern — three, then quiet, then three more — is the only stage direction in the whole song I refused to soften in rewrites. Everyone who's heard a demo asks what was in the crate. I genuinely don't know. Deciding felt like it would shrink the song.
the-diver-who-came-up-wrong: The ring shaped like a hand came from a single sentence in a salvage diver's memoir that I read once and couldn't stop thinking about for a year. I wrote the captain's offer — double pay, one more dive — before I wrote anything else, because I wanted to know exactly what temptation sounded like before I let her refuse it. There's a version of this song where she goes back down. I wrote it, sang it, hated it, deleted it. The refusal needed to cost her something real, namely the pearls, namely the money, or the whole chorus is just posturing.
what-the-lighthouse-keeper-saw: Thirty-one years is my father's age when I was born, which I didn't notice until the song was finished and I went back to change the number out of superstition, then changed it back because it felt like cheating to smooth that coincidence away. I wrote the bridge as straight prose first, no meter at all, and kept most of the cadence exactly as it fell out, gravel and all. The hardest decision was whether the keeper ever tells anyone the truth. He doesn't, not in the song, not to the swimmers he warms by his fire. I wanted the confession to only happen to us, the listener, which felt like the right kind of unfair.
the-stowaway-in-the-rope-locker: I wrote "don't even smirk" before I had a single other line, because it's the only moment of humor in a song about real danger, and I needed the narrator to have a sense of humor or the whole rescue reads as noble instead of human. The question about oceans running out like a road is a real question a child once asked me, and I've been waiting years for a song that could hold it without making it cute. I almost gave the child a name. I took it back out — she doesn't have papers in the story, so I didn't think I had the right to give her one in the song either.
the-storm-that-asked-a-fair-price: This is the only song on the record where I let myself write something close to a prayer, and it embarrassed me enough that I almost filed the whole thing under a different album. The bargain she makes is never itemized beyond "every lie I'd kept warm," because the second I tried to list specific guilts, it turned into a confession booth scene and stepped on track ten's job. I wrote the bridge spoken instead of sung because singing it felt like asking the listener to believe me, and I wanted it to sound like she barely believes herself. The flat sea afterward was the easiest line in the song and the one I trust the least.
the-confession-booth-built-of-salt: This is the song the whole record was built backward from — I had the title before I had anything else, scrawled in a notebook for probably two years. Putting the actual phrase "forty-six" back into the lyric, calling back to the cargo hold, was the first time I let one song openly cite another, and it felt almost too neat until I remembered that's what a confession is, listing the specific debts instead of just feeling bad in general. I wrote five different endings where she feels instantly clean. I kept the one where she doesn't, because relief that arrives immediately has never once happened to me.
every-name-the-sea-kept-anyway: I wrote this as a correction to the song before it, which is a strange way to build an album but it's exactly how this one happened — I finished the confession booth song, sat with it for a week, and got angry at my own premise. The idea that confession just dissolves and vanishes started to feel like a small lie I was telling the listener, so I wrote the rebuttal. The choir of centuries-old voices underwater isn't meant to be comforting in a soft way; I wanted it eerie first, comforting second, because being witnessed forever is a stranger gift than being forgiven.
the-letter-i-finally-post: Burning ten letters and keeping one was a late change — originally she rewrote all eleven, and it took my editor pointing out that rewriting everything is just hoarding with extra steps for me to see it. The clerk who barely looks up is the most important minor character on the whole record to me. I needed the act of finally being honest to be completely unremarkable to everyone except her, because that's usually how courage actually lands in the world, at a counter, stamped, ordinary. I wrote the line about courage being a stamp and a steady hand on a napkin first. Everything else in the bridge was built around protecting that one sentence.
the-water-turns-clear-past-the-wreck: The fish the color of nothing she's seen before went through six different descriptions — silver, white, gold, translucent — before I realized describing it at all was the mistake. The not-describing does more work than any color could. I wrote her laugh for no reason after weeks of writing songs about dread and guilt, and it came out so easily it scared me a little, like I'd been saving it without knowing. The line about not knowing clear water could feel like permission is the closest thing to a thesis statement this album has, and I almost cut it for being too on the nose before deciding the record had earned one honest, unguarded sentence by track thirteen.
the-choir-we-hear-from-the-deck: I wanted three different explanations on the table at once — whales, wind, grace — and never picked a winner, which felt risky in a chorus that's supposed to land somewhere. The cook and the first mate get exactly one line each because I wanted the skeptics to be ordinary and a little bored, not cynical, just busy. The detail of the captain stopping his barking at the wheel for one whole minute is the only moment of authority going quiet on the entire record, and I built the whole bridge just to earn that single image of the ship holding still together.
home-is-just-a-coast-that-waited: I went back and forth for a long time on whether to literally repeat the word "coat" from track one in the first verse, and decided the callback needed to be that plain, almost clumsy, because the album had earned the right to remind you what it cost to leave without it. No banner, no one waiting — I wrote three drafts with someone standing on the dock for her, and cut every one. The homecoming needed to be unwitnessed by anyone but the rocks, because the whole point of the record is that the witnessing already happened, underwater, months ago, and didn't need repeating here.
